Chart-Review Platform

Technical Report

Draft for review · July 2026

1Summary

Clinical chart review — reading a patient's record to answer a fixed set of structured questions — is a bottleneck in observational research. It is done by hand: a methodologist writes a rubric, reviewers read every chart, disagreements are adjudicated, the rubric is revised, and the loop repeats until agreement stabilizes. It is slow, expensive, and hard to reproduce.

This platform puts an LLM agent in front of the human reviewer as a first-drafter. For each patient the agent reads the notes (and structured EHR data where available), answers each rubric question, and cites the exact text it used. The reviewer accepts or overrides each answer. Every answer carries a machine-verified quote; every run is reproducible; and the rubric can improve itself from the reviewer's decisions.

The platform supports three kinds of review — phenotyping, guideline adherence, and named-entity recognition (NER). This report starts with the tasks themselves, in detail, because the task is what determines everything else: what questions are asked, what evidence must be gathered, and which tools the agent needs.

2The three task kinds

Every review is one of three kinds. They share the same review loop, the same evidence-citation discipline, and the same screens; what differs is the shape of the question and therefore the evidence and tools required.

KindThe question it answersAnswer shape
PhenotypeDoes this patient have / what is X?A set of structured variables (yes/no, a value, a category, a score), each evidence-cited; some derived from others.
AdherenceDid the documented care follow the guideline?A tiered set of concordance questions that roll up to a per-patient verdict.
NERWhere in the text is each entity of interest?Marked spans in the note, each normalized to an ontology concept.

Sections 3–4 develop phenotype and adherence in depth with real examples; NER is summarized in Section 5.

3Phenotype tasks

A phenotype task turns a clinical concept into a fixed list of variables to extract for each patient. The rubric defines one criterion per variable, and each criterion specifies three things: the allowed answers, the extraction guidance (how to read the chart), and the evidence that must be cited. Some variables are extracted directly; others are derived by code from extracted ones. The reviewer's job is not to fill a blank form but to confirm or correct the agent's evidence-backed draft, variable by variable.

Two examples show the range: ACTS, where the variables are many small documented facts; and RUCAM, where the agent extracts the small facts behind each scored item and the platform computes the score from them.

3.1 · Example A — ACTS (dementia phenotyping)

ACTS extracts 29 fields per note describing a dementia work-up: cognitive and mood test scores, smoking history, APOE genotype, reproductive status, allergies, and vaccines. The first thing to be clear about is which fields the agent extracts (reads from the note) and which the platform derives (computes from an extracted field):

The 22 extracted fields — the agent reads each from the note and cites a quote. Allowed values:

FieldAllowed value(s)
moca_score (MoCA)integer 0–30
mmse_score (MMSE)integer 0–30
cdr_global (CDR global)0 / 0.5 / 1 / 2 / 3
gds_stage (Global Deterioration)1–7
npi_total (NPI)integer 0–144
hachinski_score (Hachinski)integer 0–18
mattis_drs (Mattis DRS)integer 0–144
tics_score (TICS)integer 0–41
gds_depression_score (Geriatric Depression)integer 0–30
cornell_csdd (Cornell)integer 0–38
smoking_statuscurrent / former / never / unknown
pack_yearnumber 0–150
pack_per_daynumber 0–20
smoking_durationinteger 0–100 (years)
quit_timefree text — year / age / relative
apoe_genotypee2/e2 · e2/e3 · e2/e4 · e3/e3 · e3/e4 · e4/e4 · e2_carrier · e3_carrier · e4_carrier · none
impaired_cognition1 / 0
education_yearsinteger 0–40
postmenopause1 / 0
lmp_datefree text (a date)
allergenlist of records — each {allergen, category: medication/food/environment/biologic} + quote
vaccine_namelist of records — each {vaccine name, date} + quote

The 7 computed fields — the agent never answers these; code computes each from an extracted source. The exact rule:

FieldComputed fromRule
apoe2apoe_genotype1 if it carries ε2 (e2/e2, e2/e3, e2/e4, e2_carrier); 0 if a full genotype without ε2 (e3/e3, e3/e4, e4/e4); NA otherwise
apoe3apoe_genotype1 if it carries ε3 (e2/e3, e3/e3, e3/e4, e3_carrier); 0 if (e2/e2, e2/e4, e4/e4); NA otherwise
apoe4apoe_genotype1 if it carries ε4 (e2/e4, e3/e4, e4/e4, e4_carrier); 0 if (e2/e2, e2/e3, e3/e3); NA otherwise
mmse_severitymmse_score≥ 24 → normal · 19–23 → mild · 10–18 → moderate · ≤ 9 → severe
moca_severitymoca_score≥ 26 → normal · 18–25 → mild · 10–17 → moderate · ≤ 9 → severe
cdr_severitycdr_global0 → normal · 0.5 → very_mild · 1 → mild · 2 → moderate · 3 → severe
vaccine_category (projected)the vaccine_name listthe distinct Category values present across the vaccine records

So of the 29: 22 extracted, 6 derived by a rule (the three APOE flags, the three severity bands), and 1 projected (vaccine_category). A computed field is never answered by the agent — fix its source and it recomputes; if the source is null it stays Pending, never a fabricated value. pack_year shows the platform even refuses to derive where the study wants the documented figure: a pack-year is arithmetically packs/day × years, but the rubric requires the stated number, not a computed one. The extracted variables are each tricky in a different way; four representative ones:

Smoking — a dependent family of variables. Smoking is not one field but several, conditionally linked:

VariableAnswerHow it's read (all extracted)
smoking_statuscurrent / former / never / unknownThe status span. Map the phrasing ("denies tobacco" → never; "ex-smoker" → former; "1 ppd" → current). The patient's own status only — never family history. A "quit" qualifier beats residual "smoker" language.
pack_yeara number (0–150)The stated pack-year figure ("30 pack-year history" → 30). Do not compute it from packs/day × years; if only those are given, leave null. Absent → null, never 0.
quit_timefree text (year / age / relative)The cessation expression verbatim ("quit in 2015" → 2015; "age 55"; "10 years ago"). Do not normalize between formats.

This illustrates three evidence principles the whole task follows. Dependencies: quit_time only applies to former smokers, pack_year only to ever-smokers — the agent does not answer inapplicable fields. Extract, don't calculate: a stated pack-year is captured; a computable one is not. Absence is null, not zero: an unstated number is left blank so it can't be mistaken for a real measurement of zero.

APOE — one extracted source that derives three outputs. The agent extracts a single field, apoe_genotype, from explicitly documented genetic testing only: a full genotype (e3/e4, e4/e4, …), a single-allele carrier statement (e4_carrier), or none. The evidence rules are strict about what does not count — never inferred from an AD diagnosis, cognitive impairment, family history, or an ApoE protein lab value ("ApoE 4.2 mg/dL" is a serum level, not a genotype). From that one extracted field, code derives the three required outputs apoe2 / apoe3 / apoe4 (allele-present flags). The reviewer validates one evidence-cited fact, and the study gets three consistent variables for free.

Vaccines — an entity list with code-assigned attributes. vaccine_name is a list of entity records, one per vaccine actually received. For each, the agent extracts the verbatim name and its evidence span; then code assigns two attributes from reference tables, not from the model's memory: the Category (Live / Non-Live / BCG / Active Amyloid-or-Tau Immunization / Ambiguous / Not-a-vaccine) and the target Disease. Category follows a brand → abbreviation → disease precedence (the brand decides — Zostavax vs Shingrix for shingles); a disease-only mention with mixed products is Ambiguous rather than a guess. The rules also define what to exclude: only administered/received vaccines count — planned, declined, contraindicated, or merely-discussed produce no record. The model reads which vaccine and where; code decides what kind.

Postmenopause — a binary where absence means "no." postmenopause is 1 or 0. It is 1 only when the chart documents postmenopausal status or menopause; it is 0 for premenopausal status or when the topic is not addressed. The evidence rule handles both polarities: for 1, cite the smallest affirmative span; for 0, cite the section that was checked (GYN/reproductive history) so the reviewer can see the absence was looked for, not overlooked.

Across all 29 fields the same discipline holds: every extracted value carries a verbatim quote, numbers must match a number actually written in the note, and derived fields are computed rather than guessed.

3.2 · Example B — RUCAM (drug-induced liver-injury causality)

RUCAM is a phenotype task whose answer is a causality category (excluded / unlikely / possible / probable / highly probable), reached by summing seven scored items. The design choice that makes it reliable: the agent does not score the items. It extracts the small factual components each item is built from — a latency in days, a yes/no per ruled-out cause, a drug's toxicity tier — and the platform derives every item score, the total, and the category deterministically from those components. The rubric splits into 24 component leaves the agent fills and 11 fields the platform computes (the seven items, the two Item-5 tallies, the total, and the category); a leaf left blank leaves everything downstream Pending rather than guessed.

Why it is built this way: an item scored as one holistic judgment is exactly what makes a model over-score. Moving the arithmetic into the platform removes the judgment from the scoring step — the same latency always maps to the same points — and shrinks the model's job to reporting facts it can cite. The score tables live in unit-tested derivation expressions, not in the model's head.

RUCAM draws on structured EHR data through eleven read/compute tools: get_patient_summary, get_suspect_drug, get_medications, get_drug_episodes (45-day gap-merge), get_lft_series (ALT/AST/ALP/bilirubin over time), get_lab_extremum, get_serology, get_conditions, get_hepatotoxicity_category (LiverTox tier), compute_r_ratio (injury-type ratio), and score_item5_exclusion (a deterministic floor). Notes are read for anything the structured data misses or contradicts.

The seven items — each shown as the leaf fields the agent extracts and the derived fields the platform computes from them. The agent only ever writes the left column; the right column is calculated (and the platform refuses a direct write to it):

Item — what it decidesLeaf fields · agent extractsDerived · platform computes
1 · Time to onset
latency consistent with DILI?		injury_track (shared by items 1–4), onset_path, onset_latency_days (via get_drug_episodes, compute_r_ratio)item_1_time_to_onset
2 · Course after stopping
did injury improve off-drug?		dechallenge_outcome (via get_lft_series, get_lab_extremum)item_2_course
3 · Risk factors
age / alcohol / pregnancy		rf_alcohol, rf_age_ge_55, rf_pregnancyitem_3_risk_factors
4 · Concomitant drugs
another drug to blame?		concomitant_worst_timing, concomitant_worst_hepatotoxic, concomitant_attribution (via get_medications, get_hepatotoxicity_category)item_4_concomitant
5 · Other causes excluded
alternatives ruled out?		6 Group I (g1_hav_ruled_out … g1_ischemia_ruled_out) + 5 Group II (g2_autoimmune_ruled_out … g2_cmv_ebv_hsv_ruled_out) + alt_cause_explains (each yes/no, anchored on score_item5_exclusion)n_group1_ruled_out, group2_all_ruled_out, item_5_exclusion
6 · Known hepatotoxicity
known liver toxin?		hepatotoxicity_class (LiverTox tier, get_hepatotoxicity_category)item_6_hepatotoxicity
7 · Response to re-exposure
recurred on restart?		rechallenge_result (via get_drug_episodes, get_lft_series)item_7_rechallenge
Overall
score → verdict		— (nothing; both are computed)rucam_total_score (sum of the seven items), rucam_causality_category

Item 5 is the clearest case — and the source of the platform's most important lesson. Scored as one variable, the model tends to assert "all other causes excluded" and award the point, inflating the score. Decomposed into eleven named per-cause flags, each labelled (a) ruled out by test / (b) absent by note / (c) not assessed with cited evidence, "all excluded" is no longer something the model can assert in one stroke — it must set each flag, and the platform counts them. Two guardrails make this structural: a deterministic floor from structured data sets the minimum the agent must justify above with real note quotes, and the platform refuses any attempt to write an item score directly — a derived field can only be computed, never asserted. The principle across RUCAM: extract the discrete facts each item depends on, compute the score deterministically, and require cited evidence for each fact — rather than asking the model for a single holistic score.

4Guideline-adherence tasks

An adherence task asks a different question: not "what does the patient have?" but "did the documented care follow the guideline?" Two structural features make its rubric distinct. Questions are tiered — from eligibility, through whether a step was done, to how and when, to whether treatment aligned. And questions depend on each other — a later question is only asked when an earlier one fires. The individual answers roll up to a per-patient concordance verdict.

4.1 · Example — Lung cancer (NSCLC molecular-testing concordance)

The lung-cancer task assesses whether an NSCLC patient's molecular work-up followed NCCN guidance, expressed as the MT0–MT12 questions, organized by tier and each evidence-cited from pathology, molecular/genomics, radiology, and oncology notes plus structured EHR data.

#TierQuestionDepends on
MT0a0 · eligibilityConfirmed lung-cancer diagnosis (pathologically confirmed, with a date)?— (gates all)
MT11 · was it doneWas genomic (molecular/mutation) testing performed at all?MT0a
MT71 · was it doneWas PD-L1 testing (IHC) performed?MT0a
MT22 · howComprehensive genomic profiling (large NGS panel) done, on which platform?MT1
MT32 · howIf not CGP, was single-gene / targeted-panel testing done (name genes)?MT1, MT2
MT42 · howTissue, liquid biopsy (ctDNA), or both?MT1
MT52 · whenWas testing ordered before / same-day / after first-line therapy?MT1
MT62 · whenWere results available before first-line therapy began?MT1
MT7a3 · resultPD-L1 result band (TPS <1 / 1–49 / ≥50)?MT7
MT83 · resultFindings — gene + variant for each actionable result?MT1
MT8a3 · resultActionable mutation with an FDA-approved targeted therapy identified?MT8
MT94 · treatmentFor an actionable mutation, was the recommended targeted therapy given first-line?MT8a

Tiers structure the review. Tier 0 establishes eligibility; tier 1 asks only whether each step happened; tier 2 asks how and when; tier 3 records the results; tier 4 checks whether treatment aligned. The reviewer works top-down and never wastes effort on lower tiers that don't apply.

Dependencies prune the work. The depends_on links mean the agent asks "which platform?" (MT2) only if testing was done (MT1); "PD-L1 band?" (MT7a) only if PD-L1 was tested (MT7); "targeted therapy given?" (MT9) only if an actionable, druggable mutation exists (MT8a).

The verdict is about more than "was it done." The guideline's intent is that testing be comprehensive (MT2), on an adequate specimen (MT4), and — critically — available before first-line therapy (MT5/MT6), with treatment matched to actionable findings (MT8a → MT9). This is the timing trap: a chart plainly documents EGFR testing, so "was testing done?" is yes — but if the result date (MT6) falls after chemotherapy started, the guideline step was not met. Answering it correctly means comparing an order/result date to a therapy-start date — usually a molecular report combined with structured drug-exposure data, not pattern-matching the word "EGFR."

5NER tasks

The third kind, NER, marks every mention of specified entity types in the note text and normalizes each span to an ontology concept — recording the exact span and its position rather than a per-field answer. The lead project is BSO-AD (Behavioral / Social / Other determinants of Alzheimer's disease). NER shares the review loop and the evidence discipline — a marked span must sit exactly where it claims — but its rubric is an ontology of entity types rather than a list of variables or concordance questions.

5.1 · The rubric is an ontology

BSO-AD organizes what is worth extracting into nine entity-type subtrees, 660 concepts in all (ontology version 2026.05.28-0). Behavior & lifestyle and social context carry the most detail; food and health-care access are small but decisive. Every span the agent marks is typed to one subtree and mapped to a concept beneath it.

Entity-type subtreeConceptsWhat it covers (examples)
Behavior & Lifestyle205smoking, alcohol & substance use, diet, physical activity, sleep
Social & Community Context132social support, isolation, caregiving, relationships
Economic Stability96income, employment, insurance & benefits (e.g. Medicaid)
Neighborhood61housing, built environment, safety
Dementia60cognitive status & staging (MCI, Alzheimer's)
Demographic35age, sex, marital status, race/ethnicity
Education & Literacy32educational attainment, health literacy
Health Care30access, transportation to care, coverage gaps
Food9food security, meal access

5.2 · Example — one note, annotated

A single pass produces a list of spans. Below, a synthetic note (illustrative — not a real patient) yields fourteen spans across eight of the nine subtrees:

Ms. Doe is a 72-year-old retired widow who lives alone in subsidized housing in a high-poverty neighborhood. She was diagnosed with mild cognitive impairment two years ago, now progressing toward early Alzheimer disease. She relies on Medicaid, reports difficulty affording groceries and skips meals at the end of the month, and has no reliable transportation to her memory-clinic appointments. She completed only a high school education, has limited social support since her husband died, and reports feeling isolated.
SpanEntity typeConceptStatus
widowDemographicWidowedmapped
72-year-oldDemographic—novel
retiredDemographic—novel
subsidized housingNeighborhood—novel
high-poverty neighborhoodNeighborhood—novel
mild cognitive impairmentDementiaMild_Cognitive_Impairment_Due_to_ADmapped
early Alzheimer diseaseDementia—novel
MedicaidEconomic StabilityMedicaidmapped
difficulty affording groceriesEconomic Stability—novel
skips mealsFood—novel
no reliable transportation to appointmentsHealth CareTransportationmapped
high school educationEducation & Literacy—novel
limited social supportSocial & CommunitySocial_Supportmapped
feeling isolatedSocial & Community—novel

Each span also carries tool-verified character offsets (omitted above). A mapped span matched an ontology concept; a novel one is a real mention with no confident match — held for the reviewer and a candidate for growing the ontology, not a miss.

5.3 · How it runs

BSO-AD is a first-class Studio task, moving through the same phases as the others. Its extraction engine is a vendored Claude-Agent-SDK pipeline the platform drives: in TRY it runs over each patient's notes — listing the supported entity types, proposing spans, normalizing each to a concept, and resolving offsets — then writes one span record per note; in VALIDATE a reviewer accepts, re-maps, rejects, or confirms each span as novel, one note at a time. Offsets are never counted by the model: a locate_in_source tool returns the authoritative positions and the writer rejects any span whose text does not sit exactly there — the same faithfulness gate the phenotype tasks use for evidence quotes (§7).

On a small sample of real ACTS notes (nine notes, six yielding spans), the agent marked 22 spans — about 3.7 per note, across six of the nine subtrees — with roughly half normalizing to an ontology concept and half returned as novel candidates, the tail the reviewer triages. A modest run on the test-tier model: illustrative of the behavior, not a benchmark of accuracy.

6How the agent runs

For every task kind the agent is deployed the same way. On each run it receives the rubric and one patient's chart (notes + OMOP tables when present) and a tool set: shared read/write tools for every task, plus the task-specific compute/lookup tools from that task's tool profile (RUCAM's eleven; ACTS's vaccine table lookup). It runs a reason → act → observe loop — reason about what evidence it needs, call a tool, read the result, repeat — invoked one unit at a time (per note for ACTS, per component for RUCAM, per question for adherence). Two agents read each patient (a default and a skeptical reader); an optional LLM judge flags where they disagree; a human adjudicates. The agents run on Azure OpenAI (GPT-4o for test data, a GPT-5.x reasoning deployment for real PHI cohorts). Two rules are enforced, not requested: anything computable is a deterministic tool; and every write is faithfulness-gated.

6.1 · The tools, by kind

  • Read the chart: list_notes, read_note/read_notes, search_notes, get_note_section, list_structured_data, read_structured_data, list_criteria/read_criterion.
  • Compute / look up (per task): RUCAM's eleven data/compute tools (§3.2); ACTS's vaccine-name → category/disease table lookup.
  • Commit (checked): find_quote_offsets, select_evidence, set_field_assessment / set_question_answer, set_summary, set_review_status.

7Keeping it honest: the faithfulness gate

The agent cannot invent evidence. When it commits an answer, the platform verifies the cited quote is present in the note. If the text was copied faithfully but the character offsets are off, they are auto-corrected; only quotes truly absent are rejected. In the ACTS run, 100% of committed answers carried a valid quote and every numeric answer matched a number in the note — so residual errors were errors of interpretation, never fabrication. The effect for the reviewer is that they are never asked to trust a claim they can't immediately see in the source:

8Status and roadmap

The core is complete and verified end to end — three task kinds, the multi-agent loop, the judge, the run → adjudicate → validate pipeline, and the rubric-refinement loop for phenotype and adherence (roughly five to six of nine milestones). In progress: robustness/cross-view testing and end-to-end validation on the remaining projects. Remaining: a second agent provider; finishing refinement for NER; and a systematic accuracy sweep across models and settings. The near-term target is a public beta-1 — the working platform, one provider, PHI-safe, honestly documented, and proven to run from a clean download in someone else's hands.

All examples and screenshots use synthetic patients. No patient data is included.
